directory.h3: All Ford Kuga Variants - Engine Replacement Coverage
directory.intro: A full alphabetical listing of every Ford Kuga trim covered by our network. All entries link to a dedicated variant page, where prices are further broken down by engine code, model year, and condition type. Click a live link to compare quotes.

groups.title: Performance Variants
items: None. Ford Kuga does not have a dedicated performance sub-brand equivalent to Ford ST or RS.
Not sure which variant your Ford Kuga is? Enter your registration number above - we’ll identify the exact sub-model, engine code and compatible replacement options instantly.

directory.label: Variant Directory

engineGuide.h2: Ford Kuga Engine Codes - Full Technical Specifications & Replacement Guide
engineGuide.h3: Every engine code fitted to the Ford Kuga across all generations, with technical specs, compatible variants and trims, known failure points, and average rebuilt prices from UK specialists. Select any engine code to compare quotes.

entries.title: 0L Diesel
entries.history: The G6DG is a 2.0L Duratorq TDCi unit fitted to early Mk1 Kuga models, offering 138bhp and known for its robust bottom end design.
compatibleVariants: Zetec, Titanium, Zetec Business, Titanium Business
commonFailures: Turbocharger failure due to oil feed restrictor blockage (CV6W-9T509-BA)
EGR valve clogging from soot buildup in stop-start driving
ECU failure (SID206) causing cutting out or non-start issues (codes P0606)
entries.cta: Get quotes for G6DG Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 0L Diesel
entries.history: A higher-output evolution of the Duratorq TDCi, the UFMA was introduced in Mk1 facelift models with improved emissions calibration for Euro 5 compliance.
compatibleVariants: Zetec, Titanium, Zetec Business, Titanium Business
commonFailures: Turbocharger failure (similar to G6DG oil feed issues)
Injector seal failure leading to compression loss
DPF regeneration problems on short urban journeys
entries.cta: Get quotes for UFMA Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 0L Diesel
entries.history: Fitted to early Mk2 Kuga models (2013-2016), the T7MA represented Ford's next-generation Duratorq with reduced friction and improved thermal efficiency for lower emissions.
compatibleVariants: Zetec, Titanium, Zetec Edition, Titanium Edition, Zetec Business
commonFailures: Injector seal failure
EGR cooler cracks (CV6W-9F479-AA) from thermal stress
DPF regeneration frequency issues in urban driving
entries.cta: Get quotes for T7MA Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 0L Diesel
entries.history: The PSA-sourced DW10 and DW12 engines (also known as the Ford DW Series) brought common-rail refinement to the Kuga Mk2 range, shared with the Ford Focus and C-Max, ensuring parts availability and specialist knowledge across the UK.
compatibleVariants: Titanium AWD, Titanium X, Titanium X Sport
commonFailures: EGR cooler cracks requiring full cooler replacement
Turbocharger failure (AWD variants with higher load)
DPF pressure sensor failure
entries.cta: Get quotes for DW10 DW12 Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 5L Diesel
entries.history: The 1.5L TDCi XWDA was introduced in the Mk2 facelift (2015-2019) as a more economical entry point to the Kuga diesel range, offering lower running costs and ULEZ-compliant Euro 6 emissions.
compatibleVariants: Zetec, Zetec Edition, Zetec Business, Zetec Navigator
commonFailures: EGR valve clogging from low-speed urban driving
DPF regeneration failures from exhaust gas temperature sensor faults
Injector seal failure
entries.cta: Get quotes for XWDA Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 0L Diesel
entries.history: These later 2.0L TDCi units (2016-2019) feature upgraded EGR coolers and revised turbocharger oil supply systems to address earlier reliability concerns. Shared with the Ford Focus Mk4 and Mondeo Mk5, parts and specialist knowledge remain widely available.
compatibleVariants: Titanium, ST-Line, Vignale, Titanium Edition, ST-Line Edition, Vignale Edition (all available with AWD)
commonFailures: Turbocharger oil feed restrictor blockage (revised part CV6W-9T509-BA required)
EGR cooler replacement needed (updated casting CV6W-9F479-AB)
DPF differential pressure sensor failure
entries.cta: Get quotes for RFFA DV10 DV12 Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 5L Petrol
entries.history: The 2.5L Duratec ST (HYDB/HYDA) is a Volvo-sourced five-cylinder turbocharged unit fitted exclusively to the Mk1 Kuga. Known for its distinctive five-cylinder exhaust note and 197-200bhp output, it remains a favourite among Kuga enthusiasts.
commonFailures: PCV valve contamination from crankcase ventilation system
Camshaft seal oil leaks (membrane replacement required)
Turbocharger wear (approx 100,000 km interval)
entries.cta: Get quotes for HYDB HYDA Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 6L EcoBoost
entries.history: The 1.6L EcoBoost (JTMA/JQDA) was the first turbocharged petrol option in the Mk2 Kuga, delivering strong performance from a small displacement. Later replaced by the 1.5L EcoBoost in the 2015 facelift.
commonFailures: Turbo oil seal leaks
Coolant loss from cylinder head gasket failure
High-pressure fuel pump wear
entries.cta: Get quotes for JTMA JQDA Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 5L EcoBoost
entries.history: The 1.5L EcoBoost replaced the 1.6L unit in the 2015 Kuga facelift, featuring improved low-end torque and lower CO2 emissions. However, it inherited the wet belt timing system that has become the defining failure point for this engine family across all Ford models.
compatibleVariants: Zetec, Titanium, ST-Line, Vignale, Zetec Edition, Titanium Edition, ST-Line Edition, Vignale Edition
commonFailures: Wet belt degradation causing oil pump strainer blockage (critical failure from 80,000-100,000 miles)
Low-speed pre-ignition (LSPI) from piston ring land design
Coolant loss from cylinder head gasket weakness (requires OEM CV6W-6059-AB)
entries.cta: Get quotes for M1JA YYDA Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: 0L EcoBoost
entries.history: The 2.0L EcoBoost (TYDA) is the range-topping petrol engine in Mk2 and Mk3 Kuga models, offering 237-247bhp and strong torque delivery. Shared with the Ford Focus ST and Mondeo, this engine offers genuine performance potential for Kuga owners.
compatibleVariants: ST-Line, Vignale, ST-Line Edition, Vignale Edition (AWD standard)
commonFailures: High-pressure fuel pump (1847349C1) wear across all trims
Timing chain tensioner wear
Injector failure from carbon buildup
entries.cta: Get quotes for TYDA Ford Kuga engine replacement

entries.title: Hybrid (HEV/PHEV)
entries.history: The 2.5L Duratec Atkinson cycle engine powers Ford's third-generation Kuga Hybrid and Plug-In Hybrid models. Optimised for thermal efficiency rather than peak power, this engine works in conjunction with an electric motor and CVT transmission. Introduced in 2020 to meet tightening emissions regulations and growing UK demand for electrified family SUVs.

entries.title: Hybrid, Titanium Plug-In Hybrid, ST-Line Plug-In Hybrid, Vignale Plug-In Hybrid, Active Plug-In Hybrid
commonFailures: Hybrid battery cooling system malfunctions
CVT transmission integration faults
Exhaust gas temperature sensor failures (EcoBoost hybrid variants)
entries.cta: Get quotes for BGDC BGDD Ford Kuga engine replacement

commonProblems.h2: Ford Kuga Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.group: Ford Kuga 1.5 EcoBoost Wet Belt Failure
problems.h4: Ford Kuga 1.5 EcoBoost Wet Belt Failure - Why These Petrol Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Ford Kuga Zetec, Titanium, ST-Line, Vignale (2015-2024, M1JA & YYDA 1.5 EcoBoost)
problems.rootCause: The 1.5 EcoBoost uses a rubber timing belt running through pressurised engine oil (a "wet belt"). Over time, heat and oil contamination cause the belt to degrade, shedding rubber particles that block the oil pump strainer. Oil pressure drops, leading to catastrophic bearing and turbo failure.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace timing belt kit, oil pump belt, water pump, full oil system flush
repairOptions.longevity: Viable only before belt degradation causes oil starvation (typically before 80,000 miles or at first signs of rubber in oil)

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit fully rebuilt engine with upgraded wet belt kit, oil pump, turbo inspection, full flush
repairOptions.longevity: 12-month warranty; upgraded components address original belt degradation; the only safe fix once oil pressure has dropped

problems.recommendation: If you catch the wet belt failure early - before the oil pressure warning light appears or the engine starts rattling - preventative belt replacement at a specialist is the best value option. However, once the oil pump strainer is blocked and oil pressure has dropped, bearing and turbo damage is almost certain. On a Kuga worth £6,000-£12,000, a rebuilt engine from a specialist (£1,800-£2,600) is financially viable and far more reliable than attempting a post-failure belt change without rebuilding the bottom end.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt 1.5 EcoBoost engine prices for your Ford Kuga

problems.group: Ford Kuga 2.0 TDCi EGR Valve & Cooler Failure
problems.h4: Ford Kuga 2.0 TDCi EGR Valve & Cooler Failure - Diesel Emissions System Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Ford Kuga Zetec, Titanium, ST-Line (2008-2019, G6DG, UFMA, T7MA, DW10, RFFA 2.0 TDCi)
problems.rootCause: Exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) coolers crack due to thermal cycling between hot exhaust gases and cold coolant. Coolant leaks into the exhaust system or combustion chambers, while EGR valves clog with soot from repeated short journeys and stop-start urban driving.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace EGR cooler and valve, coolant flush, system bleed
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if caught early without engine damage; resolves coolant loss and rough running

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace EGR cooler, clean DPF, forced regeneration, injector inspection
repairOptions.longevity: For engines with soot buildup and DPF blockage but no internal engine damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt engine with upgraded EGR cooler design, cleaned inlet manifold, new injector seals
repairOptions.longevity: 12-month warranty; necessary if EGR cooler failure has caused hydrolock or severe cylinder contamination

problems.recommendation: EGR cooler failure is often caught early because coolant loss and rough running are obvious symptoms. If your Kuga has no misfire or knocking, cooler replacement at a specialist (£500-£800) resolves the issue. However, if the cooler has been leaking for thousands of miles, coolant contamination can damage piston rings and cylinder bores. In that case, a rebuilt engine with upgraded EGR components is the safer long-term investment.
problems.cta: Get quotes for 2.0 TDCi rebuilt engine with upgraded EGR components

problems.group: Ford Kuga 2.0 TDCi DPF Regeneration Failure
problems.h4: Ford Kuga 2.0 TDCi DPF Regeneration Failure - Short Journey Diesel Damage
problems.affectedModels: Ford Kuga Zetec, Titanium, ST-Line (2010-2019, UFMA, T7MA, DW12, DV10, RFFA 2.0 TDCi)
problems.rootCause: The diesel particulate filter (DPF) requires regular high-speed driving to regenerate (burn off trapped soot). Kugas used predominantly for short school runs and urban commuting never complete regeneration cycles. The DPF blocks completely, forcing exhaust gas back into the engine, diluting oil with diesel, and eventually causing bearing failure.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Diagnostic forced regeneration using professional equipment
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable if DPF is partially blocked and engine oil is not contaminated

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace DPF, full oil and filter change (multiple cycles), system reset
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable for blocked DPF with mild oil dilution but no engine knock

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt engine, new DPF, oil system flush, upgraded injectors
repairOptions.longevity: 12-month warranty; necessary if diesel dilution has caused bearing or turbo failure

problems.recommendation: On a Kuga used for urban driving, regular forced regenerations (£120-£200) every 20,000 miles can prevent DPF failure entirely. Once the DPF is fully blocked and engine oil smells of diesel, the risk of bearing failure is high. If the engine still runs quietly, DPF replacement and multiple oil changes may save it. But if there is any knocking or low oil pressure, a rebuilt engine with a new DPF is the only reliable fix.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt 2.0 TDCi engine prices - DPF-safe rebuilds available

problems.h4: Ford Kuga Turbocharger Failure - Oil Starvation & Wear
problems.affectedModels: Ford Kuga all diesel and EcoBoost petrol variants (2008-2024, all turbocharged engine codes including M1JA, YYDA, G6DG, DW10, RFFA)
problems.rootCause: Turbocharger failure on the Kuga is almost always secondary to another issue: wet belt debris blocking the oil pump (1.5 EcoBoost), diesel dilution thinning the oil (2.0 TDCi DPF failure), or infrequent oil changes allowing sludge to block the turbo oil feed restrictor.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace turbocharger, oil feed pipe, drain pipe, full oil flush
repairOptions.longevity: Only suitable if root cause (belt debris, diesel dilution, sludge) is identified and fixed first

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt engine with new turbo, oil pump, full system flush
repairOptions.longevity: 12-month warranty; the only safe option when turbo debris has entered the engine oil system

problems.recommendation: Turbo failure on a Kuga is rarely an isolated event. If the turbo has disintegrated, metal debris has circulated through the oil system and will destroy the new turbo within weeks unless the engine is stripped and cleaned - or replaced. Our specialist network strongly advises against "turbo only" replacements on the 1.5 EcoBoost or any 2.0 TDCi with a blocked DPF. A rebuilt engine with a new turbo and full oil system flush is the cost-effective choice in the medium term.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt Ford Kuga engine & turbo packages - supply & fit

problems.group: Ford Kuga 2.0 EcoBoost Timing Chain Tensioner Failure
problems.h4: Ford Kuga 2.0 EcoBoost Timing Chain Tensioner Failure - Petrol Performance Risk
problems.affectedModels: Ford Kuga ST-Line, Vignale (2015-2024, TYDA 2.0 EcoBoost)
problems.rootCause: The 2.0 EcoBoost timing chain tensioner relies on oil pressure. Extended oil change intervals or oil sludge buildup cause tensioner failure, leading to chain slack, valve timing errors, and eventually piston-to-valve contact.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace timing chain, tensioner, guides, cam sprockets
repairOptions.longevity: Viable preventative measure at 70,000-80,000 miles or at first rattling noise on cold start

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt engine with updated tensioner design, oil pump, chain kit
repairOptions.longevity: 12-month warranty; necessary if chain has jumped timing causing bent valves

problems.recommendation: The 2.0 EcoBoost is a strong engine, but tensioner failure is predictable. If your ST-Line or Vignale Kuga makes a rattling noise on cold start that lasts 1-2 seconds, replace the timing chain kit preventatively at a specialist (£700-£1,100). Ignoring this noise risks a £3,000+ engine rebuild. On higher-mileage examples (over 100,000 miles) with no service history, a rebuilt engine with updated tensioner components offers better peace of mind than a chain kit on a tired engine.
problems.cta: Get quotes for 2.0 EcoBoost rebuilt engine - upgraded timing chain tensioner

problems.h4: Ford Kuga Oil Consumption - Piston Ring & PCV System Failure
problems.affectedModels: Ford Kuga Zetec, Titanium (2008-2013, HYDB/HYDA 2.5 Duratec petrol), plus early 1.6 EcoBoost (JTMA, 2013-2015)
problems.rootCause: On the 2.5 Duratec, piston ring wear and PCV valve contamination cause oil consumption exceeding 1 litre per 1,000 miles. On early 1.6 EcoBoost units, carbon buildup on piston rings leads to bore glazing and oil passing into combustion chambers.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Strip engine, replace piston rings, hone bores, new bearings, PCV system rebuild
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable for engines with good compression but high oil consumption; no other damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit fully rebuilt engine with upgraded piston rings, PCV system, valve stem seals
repairOptions.longevity: 12-month warranty; often better value than ring replacement on a high-mileage engine

problems.recommendation: On a 2.5 Duratec Kuga approaching 120,000 miles with oil consumption and no other issues, a rebuild with upgraded piston rings and PCV components is a solid investment. However, once labour is factored in, a full rebuilt engine from a specialist is often only £300-£500 more than a ring replacement. For high-mileage examples, we recommend comparing quotes for a complete rebuilt unit - the warranty alone justifies the small premium.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt 2.5 Duratec engine prices for Ford Kuga

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Ford Kuga
finalCta.paragraph: Wet belt debris, EGR cooler cracks, DPF blockages, and turbo failures don't have to mean the end of your Kuga. EnginesMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Ford Kuga specialists who supply rebuilt engines with upgraded components designed to outlast the factory originals. Every rebuilt unit comes with a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty, nationwide delivery, and supply & fit options available. Compare prices today and get your family SUV back on the road without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Ford Kuga Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EnginesMarket’s UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Ford Kuga and engine code. Every engine supplied through EnginesMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineSizes.h2: Ford Kuga Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every displacement fitted to the Ford Kuga across all three generations is listed below, with compatible engine codes, UK production years, power outputs and common failure notes.

items.title: Ford Kuga 1.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.5L EcoBlue diesel arrived in the Mk2 Kuga facelift (2015) and continued into the Mk3 generation, replacing the earlier 1.6 TDCi. It was offered as an entry-level diesel option primarily in front-wheel drive Zetec, Titanium and ST-Line trims, delivering strong fuel economy for family SUV duties . This PSA-sourced DV-series unit is also found in the Ford Focus and Mondeo.
commonFailurePoints: DPF regeneration issues on short journeys
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford Kuga 1.5L diesel engines

items.title: Ford Kuga 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0L Duratorq/TDCi formed the backbone of the Kuga diesel range across all three generations, offered in front-wheel drive and AWD configurations. Initially a Ford-engineered Duratorq unit (G6DG, UFMA, T7MA), later models (2016 onwards) adopted the PSA-sourced DW10/DW12 and EcoBlue families shared with the Focus, Mondeo and Galaxy . Power outputs vary from 138hp to 190hp depending on generation and tune .
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford Kuga 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Ford Kuga 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L EcoBoost is the most common petrol engine in Mk2 (facelift) and Mk3 Kugas, replacing the earlier 1.6 EcoBoost from 2015 onwards. Available in 148hp and 178hp tunes across Zetec, Titanium, ST-Line and Vignale trims, this engine is now notorious for wet belt timing system failures that dominate UK replacement enquiries .
commonFailurePoints: Wet belt degradation blocking oil pump strainer (80,000-100,000 miles)
items.cta: Compare Ford Kuga 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Ford Kuga 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6L Ti-VCT naturally aspirated and 1.6L EcoBoost turbocharged units were fitted to early Mk2 Kugas (2013-2015) before being replaced by the 1.5L EcoBoost in the 2015 facelift. The 1.6 EcoBoost offered 148-178hp and appeared in Zetec, Titanium and early ST-Line trims, but suffered from cooling system weaknesses .
commonFailurePoints: coolant loss/overheating (cylinder head recall on early units)
items.cta: Compare Ford Kuga 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Ford Kuga 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0L EcoBoost (TYDA) is the range-topping petrol unit in Mk2 facelift and Mk3 Kugas, delivering 237-247hp exclusively with AWD in ST-Line and Vignale trims. Shared with the Ford Focus ST and Mondeo, it offers genuine performance potential but requires diligent maintenance .
items.cta: Compare Ford Kuga 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Ford Kuga 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.5L Duratec turbocharged five-cylinder petrol engine was fitted exclusively to the Mk1 Kuga (2008-2012), derived from Volvo's B5254T unit. Producing 197-200hp, it offered distinctive five-cylinder character and Volvo-derived durability, but is now becoming rare . The naturally aspirated 2.5L variant (HYDA) appeared in limited Mk1 base trims.
items.cta: Compare Ford Kuga 2.5L petrol engine prices

groups.title: Ford Kuga Hybrid / PHEV Engine Sizes

items.title: Ford Kuga 2.5L Hybrid Engines
items.description: The 2.5L Atkinson cycle Duratec engine powers the Mk3 Kuga Hybrid (HEV) and Plug-In Hybrid (PHEV) variants, introduced in 2020. Optimised for thermal efficiency over peak power, it works with an electric motor and CVT transmission. The PHEV variant offers a combined 222-225hp and an electric-only range, making it a popular choice for company car drivers . Not sure which engine size your Ford Kuga needs? Enter your registration number above - we'll identify the exact displacement, engine code and compatible replacement options instantly. All engines supplied through EnginesMarket include a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Ford Kuga specialists.

fuelTypes.h2: Ford Kuga Engines by Fuel Type
fuelTypes.intro: Choose your Ford Kuga engine by fuel type to compare realistic replacement prices, known issues, and compatible UK supply options - from the 2008 Mk1 diesel to the latest Mk3 plug-in hybrid.

items.title: Ford Kuga Diesel Engines
items.description: Diesel powered the majority of UK Kuga sales across all three generations, with 2.0-litre TDCi units dominating replacement enquiries - particularly EGR, DPF, and turbo-related failures on the Mk2 and Mk3.
items.descriptor: Diesel powered the majority of UK Kuga sales across all three generations, with 2.0-litre TDCi units dominating replacement enquiries - particularly EGR, DPF, and turbo-related failures on the Mk2 and Mk3.
families: G6DG / UFMA - early Mk1 2.0 TDCi (136-140hp), turbo oil feed failures
T7MA - Mk2 2.0 TDCi (138-148hp), injector seal failure common
DW10 / DW12 - PSA-sourced 2.0 TDCi (148-178hp), EGR cooler cracks, AWD variants
RFFA / DV10 / DV12 - Mk2 facelift and Mk3 2.0 EcoBlue (148-190hp), DPF pressure sensor faults
XWDA - Mk2 facelift 1.5 TDCi (118hp), entry-level diesel, EGR clogging
foundIn: Mk2 Facelift (2016-2019) - XWDA 1.5 TDCi, RFFA/DV10 2.0 TDCi
knownFor: Strong motorway fuel economy (45-55mpg real-world)
EGR cooler and DPF issues on short-journey urban Kugas
Turbo failure often secondary to oil starvation from other faults
High UK replacement demand on 2.0 TDCi AWD variants

items.title: Ford Kuga Petrol Engines
items.description: Petrol Kugas grew from a niche Mk1 2.5-litre five-cylinder to mainstream 1.5 and 2.0 EcoBoost units in the Mk2 and Mk3 - with the 1.5 EcoBoost wet belt failure now the single highest replacement driver for the entire model range.
items.descriptor: Petrol Kugas grew from a niche Mk1 2.5-litre five-cylinder to mainstream 1.5 and 2.0 EcoBoost units in the Mk2 and Mk3 - with the 1.5 EcoBoost wet belt failure now the single highest replacement driver for the entire model range.
families: HYDB / HYDA - Mk1 2.5 Duratec turbo (197-200hp), Volvo-derived five-cylinder
JTMA / JQMA - Mk2 1.6 EcoBoost (148-178hp), turbo oil seal and cooling issues
M1JA - Mk2 facelift 1.5 EcoBoost (148hp), wet belt degradation primary failure
YYDA - Mk3 1.5 EcoBoost (148hp), revised wet belt but same fundamental risk
TYDA - Mk2 facelift and Mk3 2.0 EcoBoost (237-247hp), timing chain tensioner wear
foundIn: Mk2 Facelift (2015-2019) - M1JA 1.5 EcoBoost, TYDA 2.0 EcoBoost
knownFor: 1.5 EcoBoost wet belt failure dominates replacement enquiries (80k-100k miles)
2.5 Duratec five-cylinder exhaust note and Volvo durability
2.0 EcoBoost offers genuine performance in ST-Line AWD
Lower UK sales volume than diesel, but rising replacement demand as diesel falls from favour

items.title: Ford Kuga Plug-In Hybrid (PHEV) & Full Hybrid (HEV) Engines
items.description: The Mk3 Kuga (2020-present) introduced Ford's electrified powertrains - a 2.5-litre Atkinson cycle petrol engine paired with an electric motor and CVT, available as both self-charging hybrid (HEV) and plug-in hybrid (PHEV).
items.descriptor: The Mk3 Kuga (2020-present) introduced Ford's electrified powertrains - a 2.5-litre Atkinson cycle petrol engine paired with an electric motor and CVT, available as both self-charging hybrid (HEV) and plug-in hybrid (PHEV).
families: BGDC / BGDA - 2.5 Duratec Atkinson cycle (HEV and PHEV variants)
Electric motor - integrated with eCVT transmission
Atkinson cycle petrol engine prioritises thermal efficiency over peak power
Electric motor assists acceleration and enables short-distance electric-only driving
PHEV offers electric-only range (approx 35 miles) before petrol engine engages
foundIn: Mk3 (2020-Present) - Titanium Hybrid, Titanium PHEV, ST-Line PHEV, Vignale PHEV
importantNotes: PHEV variants subject to high-voltage battery fire-risk recall (2019-2022 models)
CVT transmission integration means engine replacement requires hybrid system recalibration
Availability of rebuilt hybrid engines is more limited than conventional petrol/diesel
Battery health inspection strongly recommended at time of engine replacement

engineYears.h2: Ford Kuga Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: From the 2008 Mk1 launch to the latest Mk3 plug-in hybrid, this timeline covers all major engine changes across the Ford Kuga's UK production history - helping you identify your engine and compare rebuilt prices from specialists.

years.description: The first-generation Kuga arrived in UK showrooms in mid-2008, built on the Ford C1 platform (shared with the Focus Mk2) and produced exclusively at Ford's Saarlouis plant in Germany .
keyChanges: All-new compact SUV based on Ford C1 platform
Originally available with just one diesel engine option
Early cars lacked DPF on some variants (retrofit challenges)
Petrol 2.5-litre five-cylinder added from late 2008
knownFor: Strong diesel torque from 2000rpm - ideal for towing and family duties
2.5 Duratec five-cylinder engine (Volvo-derived) offers distinctive exhaust note
Early DPF systems problematic on urban-only cars
Build quality praised at launch, but some electrical gremlins reported
years.cta: Get quotes for 2008-2010 Ford Kuga replacement engines
years.ticker: Ford Kuga 2.0 TDCi turbo failure • Ford Kuga 2.5 Duratec engine rebuild • Mk1 Kuga EGR valve replacement cost
enquiries: Mk1 Kuga EGR valve replacement cost

years.description: The Mk1 facelift brought updated emissions hardware (Euro 5) and revised diesel tuning, but also introduced DPF-related complications for low-mileage urban Kugas.
keyChanges: Euro 5 emissions compliance - DPF now standard across diesel range
Revised turbocharger oil feed design (UFMA engine code)
Minor exterior refresh with LED daytime running lights
Start-stop technology introduced on manual diesel variants
knownFor: Improved fuel economy over pre-facelift cars
DPF regeneration issues on short journeys remain a pain point
Turbo oil feed revisions reduced (but didn't eliminate) failure rates
Still desirable for the five-cylinder petrol sound on Titanium X models
years.cta: Get quotes for 2010-2012 Ford Kuga replacement engines
years.ticker: Ford Kuga 2010 DPF problems • Kuga 2.0 TDCi injector seal failure • 2011 Kuga engine warning light diagnosis

years.description: The second-generation Kuga arrived on an updated C1 platform with sharper styling, more premium interior, and crucially - the introduction of Ford's EcoBoost petrol engines to the UK Kuga range .
keyChanges: Completely new body and interior design
EcoBoost petrol engines introduced (1.6L turbo)
AWD "Intelligent All-Wheel Drive" system debut
Hands-free power tailgate (kick-to-open) introduced
Ford SYNC infotainment with voice control
knownFor: Sharp, contemporary styling that aged well
1.6 EcoBoost offers strong performance but cooling system weaknesses
Powershift automatic transmission introduced - mixed reliability reputation
Diesel variants remained the UK sales volume leaders
years.cta: Get quotes for 2013-2014 Ford Kuga replacement engines
years.ticker: Ford Kuga 2013 1.6 EcoBoost engine problems • Kuga Powershift gearbox failure • Mk2 Kuga 2.0 TDCi EGR cooler replacement
enquiries: Mk2 Kuga 2.0 TDCi EGR cooler replacement

years.description: A significant under-bonnet overhaul for 2015 - the problematic 1.6 EcoBoost was replaced by the 1.5 EcoBoost (which would become infamous for wet belt failures), and a higher-output 178hp 2.0 TDCi diesel arrived .
keyChanges: 1.6 EcoBoost discontinued - replaced by 1.5 EcoBoost
Higher-output 178hp 2.0 TDCi diesel introduced (DW12)
Powershift dual-clutch auto now available on more variants
Start-stop became standard across most of the range
mainEngines: M1JA - 1.5L EcoBoost petrol (148-178hp) - WET BELT ENGINE
T7MA - 2.0L Duratorq diesel (148hp, continued for lower spec)
popularModels: Ford Kuga Titanium X 2.0 TDCi Powershift
knownFor: 1.5 EcoBoost wet belt system - UK owners now facing 80-100k mile failures
178hp 2.0 TDCi AWD offered strong towing capability
ST-Line trim introduced for sportier appearance
Powershift reliability concerns persisted on diesel models
years.cta: Get quotes for 2015-2016 Ford Kuga replacement engines
years.ticker: Ford Kuga 1.5 EcoBoost wet belt failure • Kuga 2015 2.0 TDCi AWD engine replacement cost • Kuga ST-Line 1.5 engine seizure
enquiries: Ford Kuga 1.5 EcoBoost wet belt failure
Kuga 2015 2.0 TDCi AWD engine replacement cost

years.description: The Mk2 facelift brought minor styling tweaks, but more importantly introduced the 1.5 TDCi diesel (XWDA code) and updated 2.0 EcoBlue units to meet tightening Euro 6 emissions standards .
keyChanges: Exterior refresh with revised grille and LED headlights
1.5 TDCi diesel added as new entry-level oil-burner
2.0 TDCi updated to Euro 6 EcoBlue specification (RFFA / DV10 / DV12)
SYNC 3 infotainment with Apple CarPlay and Android Auto
Active Park Assist 2 (fully automated parking)
knownFor: 1.5 TDCi offered strong economy but EGR clogging issues on urban cycles
Vignale trim introduced as new luxury flagship
2.0 EcoBoost (TYDA) gave genuine hot-SUV performance (247hp)

Kuga 2.0 EcoBlue DPF pressure sensor fault

keyChanges: All-new C2 platform - lighter, stiffer, better crash protection
Hybrid (HEV) and Plug-In Hybrid (PHEV) powertrains introduced
8-speed automatic transmission replaces Powershift on petrol models
Full digital instrument cluster available
Lane centring and adaptive cruise with stop-and-go

faq.h2: Ford Kuga Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How much does a Ford Kuga engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt Ford Kuga engine typically costs £1,600-£2,500 supply-only, with specialist fitting adding £400-£700. Main dealer prices can exceed £10,000 - far higher than independent specialists.
keyPoints: Dealer replacement cost often quoted at £8,000-£10,300 for 1.5 EcoBoost engines
Specialist rebuilt 1.5 EcoBoost supply & fit costs £2,200-£3,200
Rebuilt 2.0 TDCi diesel engines range £1,600-£2,400 plus fitting
Used engines are cheapest option at £700-£1,400 supply-only
items.cta: Compare Ford Kuga engine replacement costs from UK specialists

items.question: Is the Ford Kuga 1.5 EcoBoost engine reliable? Why is it called "Ecoboom"?
items.answer: The 1.5 EcoBoost has two well-documented issues: wet belt degradation blocking the oil pump (80k-100k miles), and head gasket failure allowing coolant into cylinders. Later 2019+ redesigned engines are more robust .
keyPoints: "Ecoboom" originally referred to 1.0 EcoBoost but the 1.5 has its own failure patterns
Wet belt debris blocks oil strainer - causes oil starvation and engine seizure
Head gasket failure allows coolant into cylinders - early signs are misfire and coolant loss
2019+ models received redesigned short block addressing these issues
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford Kuga 1.5 EcoBoost rebuilt engine with upgraded components

items.question: What are the most common Ford Kuga diesel engine problems?
items.answer: 2.0 TDCi diesel Kugas suffer three main failures: EGR cooler cracks causing coolant loss, DPF regeneration issues on short journeys (fuel dilution of oil), and turbocharger oil feed blockage .
keyPoints: EGR cooler leaks coolant externally or into exhaust - most common on 2013-2016 models
DPF regen problems on urban Kugas cause diesel contamination of engine oil
Turbo failure is often secondary to oil starvation from other faults
What Car? survey: diesel Kuga reliability scored 85% vs petrol's 93%
items.cta: Compare rebuilt 2.0 TDCi engine prices - EGR/DPF-safe rebuilds

items.question: Which Ford Kuga engines should I avoid when buying used?
items.answer: Pre-2019 1.5 EcoBoost petrol engines have the highest failure risk - wet belt and head gasket issues. Pre-2015 2.0 TDCi diesels need EGR/DPF history checked. Powershift automatic gearboxes across both fuels are notoriously unreliable .
keyPoints: Avoid 1.5 EcoBoost built before 2019 unless wet belt already replaced
2.0 TDCi diesels need verified EGR cooler and DPF service history
Powershift automatic gearbox has widespread reliability issues - stick with manual
Higher-spec Titanium/Vignale models have more electrical issues than base Zetec
items.cta: Check Ford Kuga engine prices by variant - avoid expensive failures

items.question: What is the typical lifespan of a Ford Kuga engine?
items.answer: Well-maintained 2.0 TDCi diesels regularly reach 150,000-200,000 miles. 1.5 EcoBoost petrols typically fail between 80,000-100,000 miles due to wet belt or head gasket unless preventatively maintained .
keyPoints: 2.0 TDCi diesel regularly exceeds 150,000 miles with EGR/DPF maintenance
1.5 EcoBoost wet belt requires replacement by 80k-100k miles to prevent failure
1.5 EcoBoost head gasket can fail from 13,000-150,000 km - often requires new short block
2.5 Duratec petrol (Mk1) known for 150k+ miles with regular oil changes
items.cta: Find a Ford Kuga replacement engine with 12-month warranty for your mileage needs

items.question: How do I know if my Ford Kuga needs a new engine or just a repair?
items.answer: If the engine runs but has specific symptoms (coolant loss, EGR codes, DPF warnings), repair may suffice. Once there's knocking, low oil pressure, coolant in cylinders, or metal in oil, replacement is the only reliable fix.
keyPoints: Coolant loss without overheating on diesel = EGR cooler replacement (not new engine)
Wet belt replaced before oil pressure drops = preventative maintenance, not full rebuild
Engine knocking or metal debris in oil = full replacement required
Coolant in cylinders (white smoke, misfire on 1.5 EcoBoost) = new short block needed
items.cta: Get expert advice - compare Ford Kuga engine repair vs replacement quotes

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine on a high-mileage Ford Kuga?
items.answer: For a Kuga worth £4,000-£8,000, a £1,600-£2,500 rebuilt engine is financially sensible - especially if the rest of the car is solid. On a £2,000 car, consider used engine or breaking the vehicle.
keyPoints: Specialist rebuilt engine from £1,600-£2,500 plus fitting = viable for £5k+ Kuga
Dealer's £10k quote would write off almost any Kuga - avoid main dealer for older cars
Used engines from £700-£1,400 offer budget alternative for older Mk1/Mk2 models
Always factor 12-month warranty into value equation - rebuilt provides long-term peace of mind
items.cta: Compare Ford Kuga rebuilt engine prices - see if replacement makes sense for your car

items.question: What's the difference between rebuilt, reconditioned and used Ford Kuga engines?
items.answer: Rebuilt engines are fully stripped with all critical parts replaced (best option, £1,600-£2,500). Reconditioned are tested and repaired with selective new parts (£1,400-£2,200). Used engines are removed from donor cars with no internal work (£700-£1,400).
keyPoints: Rebuilt = new bearings, rings, gaskets, timing components - 12-month warranty standard
Reconditioned = original block, new consumables - cheaper but less comprehensive
Used = low-mileage donor engine - fastest delivery but shortest warranty (30-90 days)
Remanufactured = zero-hour, factory-spec (£2,000-£3,200) - closest to new
items.cta: Compare Ford Kuga rebuilt, reconditioned and used engine prices side by side

items.question: Does the Ford Kuga 2.0 TDCi have a timing chain or belt? When must it be replaced?
items.answer: The 2.0 TDCi Duratorq uses a timing belt, not a chain. Replacement interval is 10 years or 125,000-200,000 km (approx 77k-124k miles) - whichever comes first. Always replace water pump simultaneously .
keyPoints: 2.0 TDCi belt failure destroys valves and pistons - catastrophic engine damage
Many owners mistakenly believe it's a chain due to engine sound
1.5 EcoBoost uses wet timing belt (runs in oil) - different replacement requirements
2.5 Duratec petrol (Mk1) uses timing belt - replace every 100k miles
items.cta: Get quotes for Ford Kuga timing belt replacement or compare rebuilt engine prices

items.question: Is the Ford Kuga Powershift automatic gearbox reliable?
items.answer: No - the Powershift dual-clutch automatic fitted to Kugas has widespread reliability issues. Autocar and owner forums consistently advise avoiding Powershift cars and choosing the manual gearbox instead .
keyPoints: Powershift failures are common across 2013-2019 Kuga diesel models
Repair costs often exceed £2,000-£3,000 for gearbox alone
Manual gearbox is "nicer to use and more refined" - stick with it
Some owners report transmission renewal before 95,000km (approx 59k miles)
